narrative_blocks.header: She married in Westminster, London in 1939
narrative_blocks.body: Betty Jane Burton married Robert Campbell Strong on September 1, 1939, in Westminster, London — the week before Britain declared war on Germany. She accompanied him through Prague, South Africa, Bulgaria, China, Taiwan, Washington, and Baghdad over three decades of diplomatic postings. She outlived her son Gridley, killed in Vietnam in 1968, and her husband, who died in 1999.

children.n: Private First Class Gridley Barstow Strong

narrative_blocks.header: She married in Westminster, London in 1939
narrative_blocks.body: Betty Jane Burton married Robert Campbell Strong on September 1, 1939, in Westminster, London — the week before Britain declared war on Germany. She accompanied him through Prague, South Africa, Bulgaria, China, Taiwan, Washington, and Baghdad over three decades of diplomatic postings. She outlived her son Gridley, killed in Vietnam in 1968, and her husband, who died in 1999.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother Martha was Henrietta Swan Leavitt's sister — the astronomer whose Cepheid work Hubble used to prove the universe was expanding.

institutions.institution_blurb: Beloit College B.A. 1938. Member Sigma Pi fraternity. Addressed Beloit alumni April 1967, just after leaving Baghdad, as one of the last Americans out of Iraq.

narrative_blocks.header: He passed a three-day entrance exam in 1939
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Campbell Strong passed the three-day competitive examination for the U.S. Foreign Service in 1939. His first posting was as vice consul in Prague, Czechoslovakia — the same year Germany occupied the country. He then moved to Durban, South Africa, and later Sofia, Bulgaria, before returning to Washington.

narrative_blocks.header: He was America's man in Taipei in December
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1949, just weeks after the Republic of China government retreated to Taiwan following Communist forces' victory on the mainland, Strong was named Chargé d'Affaires ad interim of the U.S. Embassy Office in Taipei. He monitored Nationalist activities, handled sensitive communications about possible U.S. military assistance, and relayed Washington's formal protests when ROC aircraft launched air raids on Shanghai. He was the face of American diplomacy to a government that had just lost a billion people.

narrative_blocks.header: Kennedy sent him to Baghdad in 1963
narrative_blocks.body: President Kennedy appointed Strong U.S. Ambassador to Iraq on May 13, 1963, just months after a Ba'athist coup had overthrown Prime Minister Qasim. Strong had written the State Department memorandum recommending recognition of the new regime. He navigated a second coup in November 1963, Kurdish insurgencies, and growing Iraqi alignment with the Soviet bloc.

narrative_blocks.header: A year after he left Baghdad
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Strong left Iraq in April 1967. On April 21, 1968 — one year and ten days later — his son Gridley Barstow Strong was killed by artillery, rocket, or mortar fire during the Battle of Khe Sanh in Vietnam. Gridley was 21 years old.

sources.short: Vietnam Veterans Memorial Wall of Faces

cross_connections.display_label: His great-aunt Henrietta Swan Leavitt's discovery of Cepheid variables enabled Hubble to prove the universe is expanding.

notable.notable_blurb: Killed in action, Battle of Khe Sanh, April
notable.primary_url_label: Vietnam Veterans Memorial Wall of Faces

narrative_blocks.body: Gridley Barstow Strong enlisted in the U.S. Marine Corps and began his tour on December 15, 1967. He was assigned as a rifleman with the 2nd Battalion, 1st Marines, 1st Marine Division. On April 21, 1968 — just four months and six days after arriving in Vietnam, and three months and twenty days after his 21st birthday — he was killed by artillery, rocket, or mortar fire in Quang Tri Province, during the Battle of Khe Sanh.

narrative_blocks.header: His great-aunt was Henrietta Swan Leavitt
narrative_blocks.body: Gridley Strong's grandmother was Martha Almira Leavitt Strong, the sister of Henrietta Swan Leavitt. Henrietta Swan Leavitt — a Hooker descendant like Gridley — worked as a 'computer' at the Harvard College Observatory and discovered that Cepheid variable stars pulse with a period directly tied to their intrinsic brightness. Edwin Hubble used her discovery to calculate the distance to other galaxies and prove that the universe is expanding.

narrative_blocks.header: His father was the U.S. Ambassador to Iraq
narrative_blocks.body: Gridley's father, Robert Campbell Strong, was a U.S. Foreign Service officer who served as Ambassador to Iraq and held other senior diplomatic posts. He was 52 years old when his son was killed at Khe Sanh in April 1968. Robert Strong died in Tucson, Arizona in December 1999 — thirty-one years after Gridley, at age 84.

bio.display_name: Private First Class Gridley Barstow Strong
bio.title: Private First Class
